item.title: Read this and flashed back to a story from Tumblr
item.content:encoded:
I was reading your story that was pretty eerie and scary. I really do feel like the “Happiest Place on Earth” could have some eerie, creepy, and certainly anomalous secrets that they wouldn’t want the public to know.

While reading the retrieval entry, I suddenly flashed back to an image from tumblr that was a “memo” for Disney Employees. Here, you might be interested in it. “A Memo to Disney Cast Members”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What with you and translucent eggs?!?
…wait… could it be?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Naaaaah, the IRL park would be too cliche.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The letter is supposed to imply that Disney, the Florida governor, the Orlando police, and the National Guard know that these things are honest-to-god monsters, but are hiding that information to preserve the tourism industry of Florida. Lines like "If it does not flinch, fire on sight." are deliberate. They're meant to distinguish the monsters from real humans in costumes, because a human being would flinch if a light was suddenly shined in their eyes. There's a conspiracy afoot, and the line about pursuing alternative legal means is deliberately meant to be an eyebrow-raiser. After all, how can you sue a monster?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I like the SCP in general, I don't like the letter at the end. If the request of the letter were to be carried out, then from the point of view of the Orlando cops and the Florida National Guard members, they're being told to murder civilians and then cover up the murders. The governor would know the cops/guards won't go for it, and whoever wrote the letter would know it too (unless whoever wrote the letter is an SCP or under the influence of one). The letter would be believable if it were, for example, disinformation from Disney in response to the Orlando chief of police asking the company if they'd lost any bullet-proof mascot costumes (or something like that).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm +1'ing alone for a Central Florida skip, because it's my duty as an Orlando native.

So, the tunnels beneath Disney World are real, though mainly they're just beneath the Magic Kingdom, and they're pretty well abuzz with attendants, techs, actors half-in and out of mascot costumes (conversing casually in profanity-laden Spanglish) and princesses half-in and out of makeup. Although they're fairly spacious and lit, during quieter hours they can get reasonably spooky, not that the parks ever truly sleep.

I dunno about St. John's River monsters (aside from dubiously-licensed speedboating rednecks) but it's just a weird river anyway, sprawling and grassy. Now, the idea of someone freaked out at seeing an alligator is a little bit absurd, but I guess if you haven't spent much time in the southeastern US, encounters with these dinosaurs-outliving jerks might actually be remarkable, despite our assessment of them as ubiquitous and lazy yet not lazy enough to stop being a serious hazard (never, ever, ever go swimming in Lake Jesup.)

Now, Gatorland…I wonder if that particular lesser-known theme park of the greater Orlando metropolitan area might be fertile SCP ground.
Good ol' Disney creepypasta is back again? Well, I have nothing to complain about. The article is genuinely creepy, and the final letter truly adds to the atmosphere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What creeps me out the most here, and it may well be the wrong interpretation, is that I'm imagining the D-class was forced into the Daisy costume for the purposes of laying the egg before being consumed…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Best part is that these horrifying Disney mascot monsters are infringing their copyright, so the entire Disney Corporation is going all out to eliminate them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the US in 1979, I'm pretty sure a PC-minded company like Disney would go out of their way to say that in a memo. 1979 is roughly around the height of the US's Second Wave Feminist movement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice one, +1
Anyone who likes this should check out the movie "Escape from Tomorrow," a black-and-white psychological thriller filmed at Disneyland without their knowledge.

item.title: Woooow
item.content:encoded:
The MTF log sold it for me.
+1
Well done sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally, a horror story involving Disney Mascots done right since A Memo to Disney Employees. +++1

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: And in less than a week! That's a new personal best that blows my old record (sub-five-weeks) out of the water!
Fuck you for doing this I love this execution I love that this is Abandoned by Disney but with a satisfying conclusion. Congrats on a well-earned 100 upvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The exploration log of the nest has a wonderful "Aliens" feel to it, which is a neat juxtaposition with the fact that the monsters are Disney mascot costumes.

Solid skip, ARD. Equal parts humor and creep.

+1. Game over, man. Game over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
in 1979, is it plausible that the note would have mentioned "the men and women" of the police department?

That they'd exist, yes, but that such a note would mention them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I came into this with a very skeptical mindset, given how overexposed 'creepy Disney' stuff, and particularly creepy theme park mascots, are. However, I'm happy to say the final addendum was a great stinger and really secured my upvote. The hinted local conspiracy and the extreme corporate doublethink expressed in the letter gave this more than enough novel flavour to bring the whole article together. A great example of how a strong final note can elevate an article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminded me instead of the "A Memo to Disney Cast Members" creepy short story (which I also really liked), so yeah Disney does just seem like a common topic.

This was well done! Though I get the feeling that further investigations would follow to isolate/capture the wandering costumes, given the nature of the Foundation…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a solid concept with great execution. I'd be scared if someone told a 5 year old me that Donald Duck eats and kills people and lays eggs too.
And in just 17 hours! I think this is a new personal best!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
predatory organisms that superficially resemble uniformed mascots

And upvote!

Seriously, there was another article about marauding amusement park mascots not long ago, if I'm not mistaken, but this is not a thing I'm going to get tired of. :D The note from Disney at the end makes it even better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So far, so good, but I'm just getting into the first test/exploration log. I gotta point out that actual radio protocol that the word "over" indicates the end of a transmission, waiting for reply. So you wouldn't say "over" after the first sentence and then say it again at the end of the transmission.

That's the only radio faux pas I see in the first one, other than both of them dropping the "over" altogether after a few lines.

Now in the Lambda-12 exploration log, there is a new thing. Shotguns really aren't measured by caliber, rather by gauge. So the "heavy-caliber" might be a low gauge, like 10 or even 8. Or it could be standard 12 gauge loaded with slug rounds. Well, you could find slug rounds for any gauge, but 12 is the easiest to maintain a standard arsenal with, since in a pinch you could scavenge ammo from pretty much anywhere.

Aside from that, this was a pretty good read. I'm glad I already avoid Disney like the plague when I can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Congratulations!

You have just reminded me of my childhood nightmare, in fictional form.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Donald Duck fetus.

A fucking Donald Duck fetus.

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude bravo, very very creepy. The fact they reproduce makes them even worse, in a good way. That's a +1 from me, captain

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There’s a large influx of Disney creepypasta, so I think it’s safe to say that Disney is just a popular company in horror since it’s so popular and kid friendly, but also has dark corporate secrets.

Like the time they forced lemmings to mass suicide for a documentary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this based off of that creepypasta "Abandoned by Disney"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
10/10 want Tales where we deal with in-park landmarks.

There's not much I wouldn't give for a grimdark, Kingdom Keepers-esque SCP tale series.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Living, quasi-organic Disney mascot costumes that haunt the utilidor system and feast on the flesh of humans???

I keep pressing the + button but it only went up once. I'm still trying though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Florida native who lived at Disney World and Ghost Tours and this skip has me shook. 10/10 want Tales where we deal with in-park landmarks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty good skip, though imo it was a missed opportunity not setting this at the irl creepy abandoned Disney Discovery Island.

On a side note, you and cyantreuse are really racing to see which Lambda-12 is gonna hit 5x3 aintcha? One of you will have to blink eventually :P

item.title: It's the only way to live (in cars)
item.content:encoded:
It's good to be back. Enjoy your stay in the House of Mouse.

Extra-special thanks to The Great Hippo for his critique and helping me polish the article, as well as doing a formatting pass of the exploration logs.

Special thanks to Mortos for his critique.

Thanks to stormfallen and TSATPWTCOTTTADC for their feedback.

See more of my work on my author page!
